turns.content: have you been paying attention to what's happening in sustainable packaging right now? like the materials side of it, not just the design. because i'm seeing some really interesting stuff coming through on projects lately

Sustainable packaging is one of the most active areas of innovation in the design world right now. The materials science side has accelerated a lot in the last couple of years — from plant-based plastics to mushroom-based packaging to seaweed films. What are you seeing in your projects specifically?

so a client i'm working with is using this molded fiber material made from sugarcane pulp. it's basically bagasse — the leftover fiber after you press sugarcane for juice. and the texture of it is incredible, it has this organic feel that you just cannot replicate with coated paperboard. it changes the whole design approach

Bagasse packaging is really interesting from a design standpoint because the material itself becomes a visual element. That fibrous, unfinished texture communicates "sustainable" before the customer even reads a single word on the packaging.

But it also introduces constraints, right? The printing behaves differently on uncoated organic fiber — ink absorption, color fidelity, the ability to do fine detail work. How are you adapting your design approach for it?

exactly. the ink absorption is totally different, you lose a lot of sharpness on fine lines and small type. so i've been designing with thicker strokes, bigger type sizes, and honestly leaning into that raw aesthetic instead of fighting it. like if you try to make molded fiber look like a glossy coated box you're going to lose. you have to design WITH the material

That's such a good philosophy — working with the material rather than against it. It's like how good web designers stopped trying to make websites look like print layouts and started designing for the medium.

The thicker strokes and larger type approach makes total sense. Are you also adjusting your color palette? I'd imagine that saturated or very light colors behave differently on that kind of surface — the fiber color shows through and shifts everything.

yeah the substrate color shifts everything warm. whites don't exist on this material, everything has a natural warm undertone. so i've been pulling my palettes toward earth tones and deep saturated colors that can hold up against the warmth. anything pastel just disappears. and for the type i've been using heavier weight cuts of typefaces i normally use in regular weight, because the ink spread on the fiber effectively adds like half a point of stroke width

That's a really precise observation about the ink spread adding effective stroke width — that's the kind of thing you only learn by actually printing on the material and comparing. Choosing heavier cuts to compensate is smart production thinking.

The color shift toward earth tones is interesting too because it naturally aligns with the sustainability message. You get this visual coherence where the material, the palette, and the brand story are all saying the same thing without being heavy-handed about it.

Are you also seeing changes in how clients approach structural packaging design? Like, are they rethinking shapes and formats because of what the sustainable materials can and can't do?

totally. molded fiber can't do sharp corners well, everything has to have a radius. so you get these softer organic forms which honestly look way more interesting than a standard rectangular box. one of my favorite things right now is how the material limitations are forcing better design. like the constraints are generating creativity instead of killing it

That's a classic design principle playing out in real time — constraints breed creativity. When you can't fall back on sharp corners and pristine finishes, you have to think more deeply about form, and the results often end up more distinctive and memorable.

The rounded, organic forms also create interesting opportunities for graphic layout. You're not just placing art on a flat rectangle anymore — you're wrapping design around curves, thinking about how typography follows a contour. It's more sculptural.

Do you think this shift toward sustainable materials is going to permanently change packaging design aesthetics, or is there a future where the tech catches up and you can get sharp corners and fine detail on eco materials?

honestly i think both. the tech will get better for sure, they're already developing coatings that improve print fidelity on fiber without adding plastic. but i think the aesthetic shift is here to stay because consumers have started associating that raw organic look with quality and authenticity. a perfectly glossy box now reads as wasteful to a lot of people. the imperfection became the premium signal which is like a total inversion of how packaging used to work

That inversion is fascinating — imperfection as a premium signal. It's similar to what happened in food branding where "ugly" produce became a mark of authenticity, or how craft beer labels deliberately look hand-made compared to the polished corporate beer brands.

For designers, this is actually exciting because it rewards craft and material knowledge over just making things look slick in a mockup. You have to understand substrates, printing processes, and material behavior — the physical reality of the design. Screen-only designers are going to struggle with this shift.

It sounds like you're genuinely energized by this area. Are you positioning sustainable packaging as a specialty, or is it just something you're encountering more naturally in your projects?

it's been more organic honestly, pun intended. clients are asking for it more and i've been learning as i go. but yeah i probably should lean into it as a positioning thing because the designers who really understand sustainable materials and can design for them well are going to be in demand. the intersection of material science and graphic design is a pretty cool place to be right now

It really is. And the knowledge barrier is actually your advantage — most designers can make something look good on screen, but understanding how bagasse absorbs ink, how to compensate type weights for fiber spread, how to design for rounded forms dictated by the material — that's specialized expertise that takes hands-on experience to develop.

If you're thinking about positioning, even a few case studies showing your process with sustainable materials could set you apart. The before-and-after of "designed for coated board" versus "designed for molded fiber" would be a compelling story for clients who are making that transition.

Good conversation — you're clearly thinking about this at a deeper level than just following a trend.

yeah i should honestly put together a case study deck or something. maybe even write up a short guide on designing for molded fiber because there's basically nothing out there. every time i searched for reference material when i started i found like two blog posts and a youtube video from 2019

That content gap is a huge opportunity. If you write even a short practical guide — ink behavior, type weight adjustments, color palette considerations, structural limitations — you'd be creating the reference that doesn't currently exist. That positions you as the person who literally wrote the guide on it, which is about as strong as positioning gets.

Plus, putting that knowledge out there attracts exactly the kind of clients you want — the ones who care about sustainable materials and are looking for a designer who actually understands the production side. It's marketing that doesn't feel like marketing because it's genuinely useful.

Start small. Even a single case study with real numbers — "I shifted from regular weight to semibold to compensate for 0.5pt ink spread" — is more valuable than a hundred generic portfolio shots.
